Z/dUdV
Z0dWdX
Z1dYdZ
Z2d[S
Base class for manipulating paths without I/O.
    PurePath represents a filesystem path and offers operations which
    don't imply any actual filesystem I/O.  Depending on your system,
    instantiating a PurePath will return either a PurePosixPath or a
    PureWindowsPath object.  You can also instantiate either of these classes
    directly, regardless of your system.
    )
_str
_hash
_pparts
_cached_cpartsc
Construct a PurePath from one or several strings and or existing
        PurePath objects.  The strings and path objects are combined so as
        to yield a canonicalized path, which is incorporated into the
        new PurePath object.
        r
_from_parts)
argsr!
__new__
PurePath.__new__c
	__class__
tupler
__reduce__
PurePath.__reduce__c
NzNargument should be a str object or an os.PathLike object returning str, not %r)
isinstancer
fspathrn

PurePath.with_stemc
Return a new path with the file suffix changed.  If the path
        has no suffix, add given suffix.  If the given suffix is an empty
        string, remove the suffix from the path.
        z

        arguments.  If the operation is not possible (because this is not
        a subpath of the other path), raise ValueError.
        z

PurePath.partsc
Combine this path with one or several arguments, and return a
        new path representing either a subpath (if all arguments are relative
        paths) or a totally different path (if one of the arguments is
        anchored).

classmethodr
propertyr
PurePath subclass for non-Windows systems.
    On a POSIX system, instantiating a PurePath should return this object.
    However, you can also instantiate it directly on any system.
    r!
_posix_flavourr
PurePath subclass for Windows systems.
    On a Windows system, instantiating a PurePath should return this object.
    However, you can also instantiate it directly on any system.
    r!
_windows_flavourr

Z.d\d]
Z/d^d_
Z0d`da
Z1dbdc
PurePath subclass that can make system calls.
    Path represents a filesystem path but unlike PurePath, also offers
    methods to do system calls on path objects. Depending on your system,
    instantiating a Path will return either a PosixPath or a WindowsPath
    object. You can also instantiate a PosixPath or WindowsPath directly,
    but cannot instantiate a WindowsPath on a POSIX system or vice versa.
    )
z$cannot instantiate %r on your system)
kwargsr.
Path.__new__Nc
_normal_accessor)
templater!
Path._initc
Path._make_child_relpathc
	__enter__P

tbr!
__exit__S

flagsr
_opener^
Path._opener
        Open the file pointed by this path and return a file descriptor,
        as os.open() does.
        rQ
	_raw_openb
Path._raw_openc
zjReturn a new path pointing to the current working directory
        (as returned by os.getcwd()).
        )
cwdk
Path.cwdc
zrReturn a new path pointing to the user's home directory (as
        returned by os.path.expanduser('~')).
        N)
homer
z	Path.homec
zoReturn whether other_path is the same or not as this file
        (as returned by os.path.samefile()).
        )
samestat)

other_str!
samefiley
Path.samefilec
zyIterate over the files in this directory.  Does not yield any
        result for the special paths '.' and '..'.
        >
iterdir
Path.iterdirc
Iterate over this subtree and yield all existing files (of any
        kind, including directories) matching the given relative pattern.
        z
pathlib.Path.globz
Unacceptable pattern: {!r}
%Non-relative patterns are unsupportedN)
auditr
selectorra
glob
z	Path.globc
Recursively yield all existing files (of any kind, including
        directories) matching the given relative pattern, anywhere in
        this subtree.
        z
pathlib.Path.rglobr]
rglob
Path.rglobc
Return an absolute version of this path.  This function works
        even if the path doesn't point to anything.
        No normalization is done, i.e. all '.' and '..' will be kept along.
        Use resolve() to get the canonical path to a file.
        FrG
objr!
absolute
Path.absoluteFc
        Make the path absolute, resolving all symlinks on the way and also
        normalizing it (for example turning slashes into backslashes under
        Windows).
        )
NFrG
normpathr
normedrc
Path.resolvec
        Return the result of the stat() system call on this path, like
        os.stat() does.
        )
z	Path.statc
        Return the login name of the file owner.
        )
Path.ownerc
        Return the group name of the file gid.
        )
Path.group
        Open the file pointed by this path and return a file object, as
        the built-in open() function does.
        )
opener)
	buffering
encoding
errors
newliner!
z	Path.openc
        Open the file in bytes mode, read it, and close the file.
        
readr

Path.read_bytesc

        rf
	read_text
Path.read_textc
        Open the file in bytes mode, write to it, and close the file.

        Open the file in text mode, write to it, and close the file.
        z
data must be str, not %s
write_text
Path.write_textc
        Return the path to which the symbolic link points.
        FrG
Path.readlinkTc
        Create this file with the given access mode, if it doesn't exist.
        N)	r
O_CREAT
O_WRONLY
O_EXCLrU
close)
exist_okrR
fdr!
touch
Path.touchc
        Create a new directory at this given path.
        T)
Path.mkdirc
        Change the permissions of the path, like os.chmod().
        N)
Path.chmodc
        Like chmod(), except if the path points to a symlink, the symlink's
        permissions are changed, rather than its target's.
        N)
Path.lchmodc
        Remove this file or link.
        If the path is a directory, use rmdir() instead.
        N)
missing_okr!
Path.unlinkc
        Remove this directory.  The directory must be empty.
        N)
Path.rmdirc
        Like stat(), except if the path points to a symlink, the symlink's
        status information is returned, rather than its target's.
        )
Path.lstatc
        Rename this path to the target path.
        The target path may be absolute or relative. Relative paths are
        interpreted relative to the current working directory, *not* the
        directory of the Path object.
        Returns the new Path instance pointing to the target path.
        )
Path.renamec
        Rename this path to the target path, overwriting if that path exists.
        The target path may be absolute or relative. Relative paths are
        interpreted relative to the current working directory, *not* the
        directory of the Path object.
        Returns the new Path instance pointing to the target path.
        )
Path.replacec
        Make this path a symlink pointing to the target path.
        Note the order of arguments (link, target) is the reverse of os.symlink.
        N)
symlink_tov
Path.symlink_toc
        Make the target path a hard link pointing to this path.
        Note this function does not make this path a hard link to *target*,
        despite the implication of the function and argument names. The order
        of arguments (target, link) is the reverse of Path.symlink_to, but
        matches that of os.link.
        N)
Path.link_toc
        Whether this path exists.
        NFT)
Path.existsc
        Whether this path is a directory.
        NF)
st_moder
Path.is_dirc
        Whether this path is a regular file (also True for symlinks pointing
        to regular files).
        NF)
is_file
Path.is_filec
        Check if this path is a POSIX mount point
        FT)
st_devr
st_ino)
parent_devZ
devZ
inoZ
parent_inor!
is_mount
Path.is_mountc
        Whether this path is a symbolic link.
        NF)
Path.is_symlinkc
        Whether this path is a block device.
        NF)
is_block_device
Path.is_block_devicec
        Whether this path is a character device.
        NF)
is_char_device
Path.is_char_devicec
        Whether this path is a FIFO.
        NF)
is_fifo
Path.is_fifoc
        Whether this path is a socket.
        NF)
	is_socket
Path.is_socketc
zl Return a new path with expanded ~ and ~user constructs
        (as returned by os.path.expanduser)
        r
homedirr!
expanduser

NNN)
F)3rE
zsPath subclass for non-Windows systems.
    On a POSIX system, instantiating a Path should return this object.
    r!
zqPath subclass for Windows systems.
    On a Windows system, instantiating a Path should return this object.
    r!
Nz-Path.is_mount() is unsupported on this systemr
